{"query":"Voyager 1 current operational instruments status 2026","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1611,"verdict":null,"extra_info":{"type":"featured_snippet","text":"As of 2026, only two instruments are operational, the Plasma Wave Subsystem and magnetometer."},"results":[{"url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Voyager_1#:~:text=As%20of%202026%2C%20only%20two,Plasma%20Wave%20Subsystem%20and%20magnetometer.","title":"Voyager 1 - Wikipedia","content":"According to current plans, they may continue operating with at least one science instrument into the 2030s.
2026-04-17 — Turned off Low-Energy Charged Particles instrument to save power (can still be reactivated). Only 2 instruments aboard operational as of now.
Voyager 1's initial orbit had an aphelion of 8.9 AU (830 million mi), just a little short of Saturn's orbit of 9.5 AU (880 million mi).
Scientists at the Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory believe that Voyager 1 entered the termination shock in February 2003.
But the magnetic field now is connected to the outside.
The fields and particles instruments that are the most likely to send back key data about the heliosphere and interstellar space have been prioritized to keep operating.
In 2023, engineers expected the spacecraft to continue operating at least one science instrument until around 2025.
On June 13, NASA confirmed that the probe returns data from all four instruments.
Between May 2025 and February 2026, Deep Space Station 43 in Canberra, Australia—the only antenna capable of sending commands to Voyager 1 and 2—was offline for major upgrades, with only limited operational windows in August and December 2025.
NASA says that: "The Voyagers are destined – perhaps eternally – to wander the Milky Way.""},{"url":"https://science.nasa.gov/blogs/voyager/2026/04/17/nasa-shuts-off-instrument-on-voyager-1-to-keep-spacecraft-operating/","title":"NASA Shuts Off Instrument on Voyager 1 to Keep Spacecraft Operating - NASA Science","content":"On April 17, engineers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) in Southern California sent commands to shut down an instrument aboard Voyager 1 called the Low-energy Charged Particles experiment, or LECP.
“Voyager 1 still has two remaining operating science instruments — one that listens to plasma waves and one that measures magnetic fields.
The team shut off the LECP on Voyager 2 in March 2025.
They are using the time to finalize a more ambitious energy-saving fix for both Voyagers they call “the Big Bang,” which is designed to further extend Voyager operations.
Tests are planned for May and June 2026."},{"url":"https://science.nasa.gov/mission/voyager/where-are-voyager-1-and-voyager-2-now/","title":"Where Are Voyager 1 and 2 Now? - NASA Science","content":"Cosmic Ray Subsystem (CRS) — Off to save power (Feb. 25, 2025) — On
Imaging Science Subsystem (ISS) — Wide-angle and narrow-angle cameras off to save power (Feb. 14, 1990) — Wide-angle and narrow angle cameras off to save power (Oct. 10 and Dec. 5, 1989)"},{"url":"https://spacedaily.com/t-voyager-1-will-reach-a-new-milestone-in-november-2026-it-will-be-so-far-from-earth-that-a-radio-signal-travelling-at-the-speed-of-light-will-take-a-full-24-hours-to-reach-it-a-distance-huma/","title":"Voyager 1 will reach a new milestone in November 2026: it will be so far from Earth that a radio signal travelling at the speed of light will take a full 24 hours to reach it — a distance humanity’s m","content":"NASA now lists the moment as Wednesday, November 18, 2026, at 2:16:07 a.m.
By April 2026, NASA’s status table listed only two Voyager 1 science instruments still operating: the magnetometer and the plasma wave subsystem.
The cosmic ray subsystem had been turned off in February 2025, and the low-energy charged particle instrument followed in April 2026.
That makes the November 2026 milestone less a triumphal banner than a sober measurement of remoteness."}]}

tavily.raw: {"query":"Voyager 1 current operational instruments status 2026","follow_up_questions":null,"answer":null,"images":[],"results":[{"url":"https://phys.org/news/2026-04-nasa-instrument-voyager-spacecraft.html","title":"NASA shuts off instrument on Voyager 1 to keep spacecraft operating","content":"Mission engineers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California turned off the Low-energy Charged Particles experiment aboard Voyager 1 on April 17, 2026. On April 17, engineers at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) in Southern California sent commands to shut down an instrument aboard Voyager 1 called the Low-energy Charged Particles experiment, or LECP. The nuclear-powered spacecraft is running low on power, and turning off the LECP is considered the best way to keep humanity's first interstellar explorer going. Years ago, the Voyager science and engineering teams sat down together and agreed on the order in which they would shut off parts of the spacecraft while ensuring the mission can continue to conduct its unique science. ##### NASA powers down two Voyager science instruments to extend mission life NASA's Voyager 1, the most distant spacecraft from Earth, is doing science again after problem. ##### NASA's Voyager 1, the most distant spacecraft from Earth, is doing science again after problem NASA powers down Voyager 2 plasma instrument to extend mission.","score":0.8014549,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://orbitaltoday.com/2026/04/22/nasa-voyager-1-instrument-shut-down-to-extend-operational-life","title":"NASA Voyager 1 Instrument Shut Down To Extend ...","content":"The NASA Voyager 1 spacecraft is running low on power, and an instrument was turned off to help keep it running.","score":0.77548623,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260504023835.htm","title":"NASA shuts down 49-year-old Voyager 1 instrument to keep it alive | ScienceDaily","content":":   Voyager 1 just powered down a nearly 50-year-old instrument to stay alive in deep space. The spacecraft is running critically low on energy, forcing NASA to make careful sacrifices to keep its mission going. Engineers are now working on a bold plan that could extend its life — and possibly revive the instrument later. On April 17, engineers at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) in Southern California sent a command to turn off an instrument on Voyager 1 known as the Low-energy Charged Particles experiment, or LECP. The spacecraft, powered by a nuclear energy source, is gradually losing power, and shutting down this instrument is the most effective way to extend the life of humanity's first mission into interstellar space. Voyager 1 and Voyager 2 remain the only spacecraft far enough from Earth to collect this kind of data. If the plan works as hoped, there is even a possibility that Voyager 1's LECP instrument could be turned back on in the future.","score":0.7663815,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.facebook.com/spacetime.mc2/posts/voyager-1-status-from-nasavoyager-1-is-healthy-and-operating-normally-in-interst/1415192043987952","title":"Voyager 1 status update from NASA","content":"With its power supply gradually fading, Voyager 1 is now running with just two active scientific instruments—one that measures magnetic fields","score":0.6565047,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://science.nasa.gov/mission/voyager","title":"Voyager - NASA Science","content":"article3 days agoWhat’s Up: July 2026 Skywatching Tips from NASA. ### What’s Up: July 2026 Skywatching Tips from NASA. * A to Z List of Missions. ### The Solar System. * The Search for Life in the Universe. article  3 days ago NASA’s Roman Launch Preparations Proceed. article  3 days ago NASA’s Dragonfly Clears Key Tests as Titan Rotorcraft Takes Shape. article  2 weeks ago Expedition 73 Crew Reflects on Science, Teamwork, and Life in Orbit. article  2 days ago Moonscapes. article  2 days ago Revealing the Invisible. article  3 days ago A Day of Flight Testing at NASA Armstrong. article  4 days ago NASA’s New Horizons Spacecraft Wakes from Hibernation in Good Health. Voyager 1 and its twin Voyager 2 are the only spacecraft ever to operate outside the heliosphere, the protective bubble of particles and magnetic fields generated by the Sun. Voyager 1 reached the interstellar boundary in 2012, while Voyager 2 (traveling slower and in a different direction than its twin) reached it in 2018.","score":0.59457535,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":1.14,"request_id":"583c5e44-8257-4731-a64c-485e200736be"}
